does: This paper develops a domain-centered benchmark evaluation framework for generative AI in journalism through human-centered design. The authors conducted a workshop with 23 journalism professionals to understand real-world use cases and challenges, then created an evaluation cookbook that translates domain-specific tasks into concrete assessment constructs. The work identifies key tensions in benchmark design, including how to align metrics with professional values, accommodate diverse stakeholder needs, and ensure evaluations capture authentic workplace practices rather than abstract capabilities. The paper moves beyond generic benchmarks by grounding assessment in the actual contexts where practitioners deploy AI systems.
connects: This work builds on long-standing critiques of AI benchmarks for lacking ecological and construct validity, drawing particularly on HCI and human-centered design methodologies that emphasize user involvement in technology evaluation. It extends prior research showing that generic benchmarks often misalign with real-world requirements, and responds to the problem that existing generative AI evaluations frequently fail to capture domain-specific workflows, values, and constraints. The paper engages with literature on measurement validity in social science and applies these principles to AI assessment, recognizing that different professional communities have distinct evaluation priorities and constraints that standardized benchmarks cannot address.
enables: This approach establishes a replicable methodology for creating domain-specific evaluation frameworks that other professional communities—legal, medical, scientific research, creative industries—could adapt. By making benchmark design more participatory and grounded in actual practice, the work enables more meaningful assessment of whether AI systems genuinely support practitioner workflows rather than gaming narrow metrics. The journalism evaluation cookbook provides practitioners direct tools to assess models in their own contexts, pointing toward a future where benchmarks are co-designed with domain experts rather than imposed externally. This work also supports more honest conversations between technology companies and professional communities about AI capabilities and limitations.
